content.problemStatement:
You have a tree with NNN vertices rooted at vertex 111.

Color each vertex of the tree either red or blue such that the expression

‚ą£cR‚ąícB‚ą£+‚ą£sR‚ąísB‚ą£|c_R - c_B| + |s_R - s_B|‚ą£cR‚Äč‚ąícB‚Äč‚ą£+‚ą£sR‚Äč‚ąísB‚Äč‚ą£is minimized, where:


cRc_RcR‚Äč is the number of vertices colored red, and cBc_BcB‚Äč is the number of vertices colored blue.
sRs_RsR‚Äč is the score of the color red, and sBs_BsB‚Äč is the score of the color blue. These can be computed as follows:

Consider some vertex uuu (1‚Č§u‚Č§N1 \leq u \leq N1‚Č§u‚Č§N), and look at the subtree‚Ä†^\dagger‚Ä† of uuu.
If there are strictly more red vertices than blue vertices in this subtree, add 111 to the score of red.
Otherwise, if the number of red vertices and blue vertices in the subtree of uuu are equal, and uuu is colored red, add 111 to the score of red.
Otherwise, add 111 to the score of blue.


Find any coloring of the tree that **minimizes** the above expression.

‚Ä†^\dagger‚Ä† Vertex vvv is said to lie in the subtree of vertex uuu, if the unique simple path from 111 to vvv in the tree contains uuu.

In particular, uuu lies within its own subtree.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚ÄĒ the number of vertices
in the tree.
The next N‚ąí1N-1N‚ąí1 lines describe the edges. The iii-th of these N‚ąí1N-1N‚ąí1 lines contains two space-separated integers uiu_iui‚Äč and viv_ivi‚Äč, denoting an edge between uiu_iui‚Äč and viv_ivi‚Äč.




Output Format
For each test case, output a string of length NNN containing only the characters `R` and `B`, where the iii-th of these characters denotes the color of the iii-th node of the tree.


If there are multiple possible colorings that minimize ‚ą£cR‚ąícB‚ą£+‚ą£sR‚ąísB‚ą£|c_R - c_B| + |s_R - s_B|‚ą£cR‚Äč‚ąícB‚Äč‚ą£+‚ą£sR‚Äč‚ąísB‚Äč‚ą£, you may print any of them.


Note that the output **is case-sensitive**.
constraints: The input edges describe a tree on NNN vertices
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2‚čÖ1052\cdot 10^52‚čÖ105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: With the given coloring, cR=cB=1c_R = c_B = 1cR‚Äč=cB‚Äč=1. Now,

In node 111's subtree, the number of nodes colored red (111) is equal to the number of nodes colored blue (111). 111 is colored blue, so we add 111 to sBs_BsB‚Äč.
In node 222's subtree, the number of nodes colored red (111) is greater than the number of nodes colored blue (000), so we add 111 to sRs_RsR‚Äč.

So, ‚ą£cR‚ąícB‚ą£+‚ą£sR‚ąísB‚ą£=‚ą£1‚ąí1‚ą£+‚ą£1‚ąí1‚ą£=0|c_R - c_B| + |s_R - s_B| = |1-1| + |1-1| = 0‚ą£cR‚Äč‚ąícB‚Äč‚ą£+‚ą£sR‚Äč‚ąísB‚Äč‚ą£=‚ą£1‚ąí1‚ą£+‚ą£1‚ąí1‚ą£=0, which is clearly the minimum possible value.
Test case 222: For the given coloring, cR=3c_R = 3cR‚Äč=3 and cB=2c_B = 2cB‚Äč=2. Now,

For node 111's and 222's subtree: red wins, so sR=2s_R = 2sR‚Äč=2.
For node 333's, 444's and 555's subtree: blue wins, so sB=3s_B = 3sB‚Äč=3.

This gives us ‚ą£3‚ąí2‚ą£+‚ą£2‚ąí3‚ą£=1+1=2|3-2| + |2-3| = 1+1 = 2‚ą£3‚ąí2‚ą£+‚ą£2‚ąí3‚ą£=1+1=2.
It can be shown that attaining a lower value is not possible no matter how we color the vertices.
